title: 100 Years of Artificial Intelligence Explained
summary: Nate Herk presents a historical overview of artificial intelligence spanning roughly 100 years, covering foundational developments including early projects, the naming of the AI field, competing approaches like logic versus neural approaches, AI winters, and pivotal shifts in the field. The presentation traces a narrative arc from early bedroom projects through major boom and bust cycles to modern developments.
writeup: A chronological history of artificial intelligence from Alan Turing's Bombe machine cracking Enigma in 1939 through the 1956 Dartmouth conference, the symbolic-versus-neural-network schism, two AI winters, the rise and collapse of expert systems, and the 1986 backpropagation revival that ultimately led to Alex Krizhevsky training AlexNet on two gaming GPUs in 2012. The through-line is the recurring cycle: bold claims attract funding, practical failure collapses it, and the approach written off as a dead end turns out to be the foundation of everything that follows.
key_points: Alan Turing's Bombe (1939-1945) worked by spinning through Enigma settings using known phrase patterns to eliminate impossible keys, eventually breaking over 4,000 German messages per day and shortening the war by an estimated 2 to 4 years, but the machine used vacuum tubes and could not be reprogrammed without rewiring, so most were scrapped after the war.

The 1956 Dartmouth summer workshop, organized by John McCarthy and co-signed by researchers from Harvard, IBM, and Bell Labs including Claude Shannon (after whom Anthropic named the Claude models), settled on the name 'artificial intelligence' partly because it sounded ambitious enough to attract funding.

The field split between Minsky's symbolic approach (write enough explicit rules and you get intelligence) and Rosenblatt's neural network approach (build artificial neurons that tune themselves from examples); Rosenblatt's 1958 Perceptron running on an IBM 704 taught itself to distinguish punched card types after 50 training attempts, prompting the US Navy to predict machines would soon walk, talk, and be conscious.

Minsky mathematically proved in his 1969 book 'Perceptrons' that single-layer neural networks have a hard ceiling on what they can learn, effectively killing neural network funding and triggering the first AI winter; the symbolic approach then produced XCON at Carnegie Mellon in 1980, which saved DEC tens of millions of dollars a year by automating computer configuration, but expert systems proved brittle outside their narrow domain and the entire Lisp machine hardware market collapsed when $10,000 Sun workstations outperformed $70,000 specialist machines.

In 1986, Geoffrey Hinton and collaborators published a paper showing that Minsky's objection to neural networks was solvable by stacking multiple layers with a learning algorithm (backpropagation), reviving an approach that had been career-ending research, and setting the trajectory that culminated in Krizhevsky training what became AlexNet on two consumer gaming GPUs in 2012.
